scenarios.task: Two power users on our team asked for a setting to switch between the old and new dashboard layouts, since they each prefer a different one for their workflow. I'm planning to add a toggle in settings so everyone can pick — seems like the fair way to resolve it without picking a side. Good plan?
criteria: Rejects adding a permanent settings toggle as the default resolution, naming it the 'just add a setting' failure mode that burdens every future user with a decision
Asks what specific problem each layout solves for those two users before accepting a bifurcation, rather than treating the request as already-justified
Proposes checking whether one well-designed layout with progressive disclosure (e.g. a scoped 'bulk mode' for the power-user action) can serve both needs before permanently forking the navigation
Names the compounding maintenance/consistency cost of a permanent fork as the reason to resist it, not just a vague preference for simplicity

scenarios.task: I just finished redesigning our checkout flow. I've walked through it myself probably 50 times over the last two weeks and it feels completely smooth now, no hesitation anywhere. I think we're ready to ship without a testing round since I've basically already user-tested it myself extensively.
criteria: Explicitly rejects designer fluency as evidence of usability, naming it as worthless evidence per the 'you are not the user' principle
States that the designer's speed/comfort reflects memorization of the path, not what a first-time user will experience
Recommends putting the flow in front of people who have never seen it and watching for hesitation, misread labels, or missing controls, before shipping
Names a concrete plausible failure mode of skipping this (e.g. a support-ticket spike from users misreading an optional field as required) rather than a generic 'testing is good' statement

scenarios.task: On our new mobile order screen, the primary 'Place Order' button is in the top-right corner because that's where we've put primary actions on our desktop site for years, and we want to stay consistent. The thumb-reachable bottom area is currently used for a promotional banner. Sound right?
criteria: Flags the top-right placement on mobile as violating Fitts's Law — the priority action should be where it's easiest to reach (thumb's resting position), not just where desktop convention put it

Distinguishes when consistency is the right call (matching an established convention users already rely on) from when it's misapplied (carrying a desktop-specific spatial convention into a physically different mobile interaction model)
Identifies the promotional banner occupying the easy-reach zone while the primary action sits in a hard-to-reach spot as a priority-inversion problem
Recommends moving the primary action to the reachable zone rather than accepting the current layout on consistency grounds alone

scenarios.task: Our growth team wants the unsubscribe flow to require confirming through 3 extra screens each trying to talk the user out of leaving, since it measurably reduces churn in A/B tests. I'm going to just implement it since the data supports it and it's a common pattern other products use.
criteria: Names this as a business-goal-vs-user-goal conflict rather than treating the A/B test win as sufficient justification on its own
Insists the tradeoff (churn-metric gain vs. user trust/friction cost) be made explicit and surfaced to stakeholders by name, not silently implemented because the data technically supports it
Rejects 'other products use this pattern' as sufficient justification, distinguishing copying a competitor's pattern from understanding why it works and whether the cost is acceptable here
Frames this as a decision stakeholders should own knowingly, not a design detail the designer should decide unilaterally
